text: The surgeon inserts a ten millimeter port using a direct cut down technique, followed by the placement of two to three five millimeter ports.
A fixed liver retractor with a ratcheted grasper is inserted into the surgical field, visibly positioning the liver for better access.
The surgeon uses a diathermy scissor to cut the coronary triangular ligaments or side attachments, manipulating the instrument with one hand.
The surgeon uses diathermy scissors, a surgical instrument designed for cutting tissue with electrical current, to perform precise dissection during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon scores the peritoneum to mobilize the colon away from the Gerota's fascia, ensuring not to penetrate or cut into the fascia during the process.
The surgeon exposes the geratous fascia, which appears salmon pink, contrasting with the egg yellow color of the adjacent mesentery.
The surgeon continues the dissection by finding the anatomical plane and maneuvering through it, maintaining separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon navigates through a relatively avascular plane, maintaining dissection within the correct anatomical layer to facilitate quick progress.
The surgeon carefully avoids entering Gerota's fascia, except near the hilum, where it is lifted to expose the psoas muscle.
The surgeon exposes the duodenum, preparing to perform a careful Kocher maneuver with minimal use of cautery.
The duodenum is shown after being carefully mobilized, with sharp dissection being primarily used to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection around the duodenum in an a-thermal manner, carefully avoiding serosal tears and minimizing the risk of injury.
The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly to fully mobilize the bowel, ensuring careful handling of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon dissects on the left side to expose the aorta, ensuring its location is visible during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a fixed retractor to hold tissues in place. A laparoscopic peanut is visible, but the surgeon prefers using a metal pointer for its durability and cost-effectiveness.

The surgeon uses scissors to carefully dissect around the renal vein, avoiding blind spreading with right angles to prevent tearing posterior branches. The vessels are dissected cleanly to minimize the risk of hemlock failure.
The surgeon identifies the renal vein and renal artery, noting the presence of an accessory artery with two branches: a main branch located posteriorly and a smaller branch anteriorly.

The surgeon identifies and dissects a smaller branch of the renal artery without using a right-angle instrument, carefully grasping the artery to avoid excessive force that could cause avulsion, particularly in older patients with less mobile arteries.
The surgeon carefully places Hem-o-lok clips on the opposite side of the dissected vessels, ensuring not to apply excessive force that could avulse the vessel from the aorta.

The surgeon places two Hem-o-lok clips on the stay side of the main artery, revealing the open lumen of the artery. Occasionally, a stapler is used depending on the anatomical configuration, but Hem-o-lok clips are generally preferred.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the renal vein, ensuring visibility of the entire vein while cautiously exploring for any additional accessory arteries.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree camera to visualize the area behind the renal vein, carefully dissecting surrounding tissues without a right-angle instrument. Gold Hem-o-lok clips are applied to secure vessels away from the renal vein.

The surgeon carefully adjusts the placement of the hemlock clip to ensure it does not lock onto the renal artery or vein. The clip is positioned tightly, but securely, with an emphasis on leaving a tissue cuff on the stay side by cutting close to the distal hemlock.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum between the liver and the kidney, preparing for the adrenal sparing portion of the nephrectomy.

The surgeon adjusts the diathermy settings to prevent bleeding while dissecting near the adrenal gland. If bleeding occurs, hemlocks are applied across the top of the adrenal. A stapler may be used for quick hemostasis, although a harmonic device is typically avoided.

The surgeon identifies and clips small vessels from the renal artery to the adrenal gland to prevent bleeding. They note the presence of a blood vessel at the top corner that requires attention. The surgeon mobilizes the kidney by freeing the upper pole before proceeding to the lower pole, ensuring the posterior dissection is complete before starting the lateral dissection.
The surgeon uses natural attachments to maintain the position of the kidney, preventing it from falling downward, which would require additional retraction effort.

The surgeon identifies veins crossing the surgical field, noting that the gonadal vein has been taken higher up as it drains directly into the renal vein. High-energy coagulation is used for efficient and bloodless dissection, with settings adjusted between fifty to sixty for this section. The surgeon emphasizes maintaining the correct plane within Girota's fascia to avoid bleeding and ensure efficient dissection.
